# Italian review worklist — un puente al giorno

**Derived from the bank. Do not edit this file** — corrections go into the entry
JSON (or tell the editor), then this file is regenerated. Exam relevance: CILS oral safety is a selling point — check the formality tags.

106 active entries · 99 need attention first · generated 2026-07-19

The five checks per entry: is the expression natural, is the meaning accurate, is
the example something a native would say, are register/regions honest, and does
the cited page actually support the entry (open the URL).

## Needs attention first (99)

### 0001 — Avere la testa tra le nuvole

- **lit.** "to have one's head among the clouds"
- **meaning:** To be absent-minded; not paying attention.
- **example:** Oggi non riesco a concentrarmi, ho la testa tra le nuvole.
  — *I can't focus today, my head's in the clouds.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/ricerca/avere+la+testa+tra+le+nuvole/ (headword: nuvola) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **trap when producing Italian:** Italian takes avere here, not essere — ho la testa tra le nuvole.
- **notes:** Source spot-checked in Treccani; RAE entry not opened. Direction of warning assigned es→it on import; reviewer to confirm.

### 0002 — Essere fuori come un balcone

- **lit.** "to be out like a balcony"
- **meaning:** To be out of one's mind.
- **example:** Ma sei fuori come un balcone? Non puoi guidare così!
  — *Are you out of your mind? You can't drive like that!*
- **register:** slang · **exam:** avoid_in_exam · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Corpus → https://slengo.it/define/fuori-come-un-balcone (headword: fuori come un balcone) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** Colloquial/regional (strong in Emilia and Rome). NOT in Treccani. Fine among friends; wrong for a CILS oral.
- **notes:** THE CAUTIONARY ENTRY. Shipped unflagged in the personal app. IT side is slang and dictionary-absent (source is a slang dictionary, mapped to authority 'Corpus'); ES side is Peninsular-only. Both problems must be resolved, not just documented. 2026-07-17: Europass list surfaced 'essere una capra' (IT) = to be IGNORANT — a false friend of this entry's ES side (como una cabra = crazy). Same goat, different defect. Consider a cross-language note or companion entry.

### 0028 — Rivoltare la frittata

- **lit.** "to flip the frittata"
- **meaning:** To spin a situation to make oneself look better; to twist an argument to dodge blame.
- **example:** Ha sbagliato lui, ma ha rivoltato la frittata e ora sembra colpa mia.
  — *He was the one who made the mistake, but he flipped the frittata and now it looks like it's my fault.*
- **register:** colloquial · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/frittata/ (headword: frittata) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **trap when producing Italian:** Using 'rivoltare la frittata' for a genuine comeback misfires badly — you are accusing yourself of spin. For the sports-comeback sense Italian says 'ribaltare la situazione' or 'ribaltare il risultato'.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-13. Meaning divergence is the payload. Reviewer to confirm: (a) MX surface form, (b) whether ES side can also carry the negative 'spin' sense in some regions, (c) 'ribaltare la situazione' as the recommended IT alternative.

> reviewer: [ ] expression is natural  [ ] meaning accurate  [ ] example natural  [ ] register/regions right  [ ] source opened & confirms

### 0029 — C'era una volta

- **lit.** "there was a time/turn"
- **meaning:** Once upon a time — the fixed opener of fairy tales and stories.
- **example:** C'era una volta un re che aveva tre figlie.
  — *Once upon a time there was a king who had three daughters.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/volta1/ (headword: volta) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **trap when producing Italian:** The haber→avere cognate pull produces 'aveva una volta' or 'ha molte cose' for existentials. Italian existentials take esserci: c'è, c'era. If you can say c'era una volta, you already own the pattern — lean on it.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14 (as 'Habia una vez / cera una volta'; forms normalised to 'Había' and 'C'era'). CLASSIFICATION: typed idiom, not formula — the schema defines formula as a fixed SOCIAL phrase tied to an occasion (buon anno), which is why formulas require pins. A narrative opener has no occasion; it is a fixed discourse idiom. Reviewer may overrule.

### 0031 — Magari

- **lit.** "blessed (from Greek makári)"
- **meaning:** If only; I wish — and, separately, 'maybe'.
- **example:** Magari venisse domani!
  — *If only she came tomorrow!*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/magari/ (headword: magari) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **trap when producing Italian:** Hearing magari as pure ojalá misparses half its uses: 'magari vengo domani' is 'maybe I'll come', not a wish. Context and mood decide — indicative magari is 'maybe', subjunctive magari is a wish.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14. Etymology pair (Arabic vs Greek) is a card-front gift. Exclamative 'Magari!' = 'I wish!' worth a reviewer check as third sense.

### 0032 — Cioè

- **lit.** "that is (ciò + è)"
- **meaning:** That is; I mean — the conversational rephraser.
- **example:** Arriva venerdì, cioè dopodomani.
  — *She arrives Friday — that is, the day after tomorrow.*
- **register:** colloquial · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/cioe/ (headword: cioè) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **trap when producing Italian:** The calque 'o sia' lands on a real Italian word — ossia — but the wrong one: ossia is formal, written 'or rather'. The conversational filler is cioè.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14. The o sea→ossia near-miss (real word, wrong register) is the subtlest trap in the bank so far.

### 0036 — Rendere pan per focaccia

- **lit.** "to give back bread for focaccia"
- **meaning:** To pay someone back in kind — usually with interest.
- **example:** Mi ha fatto uno sgarbo e io gli ho reso pan per focaccia.
  — *He slighted me and I gave as good as I got.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** De Mauro → https://dizionario.internazionale.it/parola/rendere-pan-per-focaccia (headword: rendere pan per focaccia) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** Submitted as 'vendere pan per focaccia' glossed 'pass something off as better than it is'. Web-verified 2026-07-16 against De Mauro/Treccani/Wikipedia: the canonical verb is RENDERE and the meaning is repaying a wrong with equal or GREATER harshness (Boccaccio, Decameron VIII.8). The submitted gloss matches the ETYMOLOGY (the two breads' unequal worth; originally even a positive neighborly-exchange sense) but not modern usage. A human should still open the De Mauro page before approval — web verification is not the sign-off.
- **trap when producing Italian:** Calquing gives 'pagare con la stessa moneta' — real Italian, but flat; the idiomatic form is rendere pan per focaccia, and it threatens MORE than you got, not the same. Note the clipped 'pan' inside the fixed phrase.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-16 from his Italian teacher contact as 'vendere...'. Verb and meaning corrected and web-verified same day (see it.regionNote). Teacher's gloss traced to real etymology — an interesting error, not a careless one. IT reviewer: confirm 'with interest' nuance vs exact-change 'misma moneda'.

> reviewer: [ ] expression is natural  [ ] meaning accurate  [ ] example natural  [ ] register/regions right  [ ] source opened & confirms

### 0037 — Mal comune, mezzo gaudio

- **lit.** "common misfortune, half joy"
- **meaning:** A trouble shared is a trouble halved — company in misery genuinely comforts.
- **example:** Abbiamo perso tutti — mal comune, mezzo gaudio.
  — *We all lost — a trouble shared is a trouble halved.*

### 0038 — A occhio e croce

- **lit.** "by eye and cross"
- **meaning:** Roughly; at a guess; approximately.
- **example:** A occhio e croce ci vorranno due ore.
  — *At a rough guess it'll take two hours.*
- **register:** colloquial · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/croce/ (headword: croce (locuz. «a occhio e croce»)) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** Kitchen split PARTLY RESOLVED: Treccani glosses the locuzione with estimating verbs — stimare, giudicare, valutare — and De Mauro's example is guessing someone's age, both supporting Claude's claim that 'a occhio e croce' leans toward estimates of quantity, time and value. Whether plain 'a occhio' is the natural kitchen form (metti il sale a occhio) is still unattested here — Italian reviewer to settle.
- **trap when producing Italian:** Calquing the cooper — 'all'occhio del buon bottaio' — is nonsense in Italian. In the kitchen say a occhio; for estimates, a occhio e croce.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-16 from Mexican usage (cooking context). IT pairing chosen by Claude. 2026-07-18 CORRECTIONS: (1) etymology — Claude's 'Milanese silk weavers' contained invented detail; the loom/warp-thread origin and Latin ad oculum are corroborated by Wikipedia and Virgilio Sapere, note rewritten. (2) SOURCE URL WAS WRONG — Claude had cited Treccani's 'occhio' entry; the locuzione is listed under CROCE. Now cites croce, plus De Mauro's dedicated headword. Both still unopened by a human: verifiedOn stays null. IT reviewer: confirm the kitchen/estimate split, the only claim still unsupported.

### 0039 — In bocca al lupo

- **lit.** "into the wolf's mouth"
- **meaning:** Good luck — with a ritual reply: never grazie, always 'crepi (il lupo)'.
- **example:** —Domani ho l'esame. —In bocca al lupo! —Crepi!
  — *“My exam's tomorrow.” “Good luck!” “Thanks!” (lit. may it die!)*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/lupo/ (headword: lupo) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **trap when producing Italian:** Two stumbles: calquing suerte gives 'buona fortuna', which superstitious Italians sidestep — and answering grazie to in bocca al lupo breaks the ritual. The reply is crepi.
- **notes:** Discovered via FluentU list (Joe, 2026-07-16). FIRST PAIRING REJECTED by editor same day: '¡Mucha mierda!' (theatre, vulgar) was judged both crude and a mismatch for Italy's universal wish — correct on both counts. Re-paired with the true everyday counterpart; the ritual/no-ritual asymmetry is now the payload. IT reviewer to confirm crepi etiquette framing.

- **notes:** Prompted by an inspirational graphic Joe shared 2026-07-18 ('el agua no sonaría tan hermosa sin piedras en su camino' — a modern composed line, not a refrán, so not bankable itself). Both proverbs and the universal-vs-partial reading are Claude's; the scope difference is the payload and needs both reviewers to confirm it is real rather than an artifact of how each is usually quoted.

### 0063 — Chi ha tempo non aspetti tempo

- **lit.** "whoever has time, let them not wait for time"
- **meaning:** If you have the chance now, don't wait for a better moment.
- **example:** Firmalo adesso: chi ha tempo non aspetti tempo.
  — *Sign it now — if you can do it today, don't wait.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/tempo/ (headword: tempo) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** The everyday twin of the Spanish is 'non rimandare a domani quello che puoi fare oggi'. This older proverb is the more idiomatic Italian and is paired here deliberately; the literal twin is recorded as an alternative pairing.
- **trap when producing Italian:** The word-for-word Italian version exists and is understood, but this older proverb is the idiomatic one — and note the subjunctive: non ASPETTI, not 'non aspetta'.
- **notes:** Prompted by Joe sharing the English aphorism 'life is not a rehearsal' 2026-07-18 (authored, no fixed counterpart, not bankable itself). IT side deliberately NOT the literal twin — that pairing is filed in alternatives.json as A005. Reviewer to confirm 'chi ha tempo non aspetti tempo' is still current speech rather than dictionary-only, and to sanity-check the subjunctive form.

### 0080 — Ho caldo

- **lit.** "I have hot"
- **meaning:** I'm hot (temperature).
- **example:** Ho caldo, apri la finestra.
  — *I'm hot, open the window.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** it · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Treccani → https://www.treccani.it/vocabolario/caldo/ (headword: caldo) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **trap when producing Italian:** An English-influenced Spanish speaker who has learned to avoid 'estoy caliente' sometimes still produces 'sono caldo' in Italian. Same trap, same fix: ho caldo.
- **notes:** Kept by editorial decision: technically a collocation, but idiomatic in the sense that matters (HAVE for body states, not BE). Weak as a headline entry — its earning power is the trap, not the expression: estoy caliente / sono caldo both read as arousal. Slot as a summer-season beginner day, never a showcase. Note: schema CEFR floor is A2; there is no A1 value.

### 0231 — da solo

- **lit.** "from alone"
- **meaning:** Alone; by oneself.
- **example:** L'ha fatto da solo.
  — *He did it by himself.*

- **source to verify:** De Mauro → https://dizionario.internazionale.it/parola/solo (headword: solo) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN

### 0237 — alla fine

- **lit.** "at-the end"
- **meaning:** In the end; finally.
- **example:** Alla fine ha accettato.
  — *In the end he accepted.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** it · **status:** draft

- **trap when producing Italian:** Gender bites: Italian LA fine (feminine) vs Spanish EL final (masculine) — so the contraction differs, alla vs al. And Italian il finale (masc.) means the ending of a film; la fine is the end of things.
